explanation:
**Normal health values** come from large-scale population studies (NHANES, WHO, CDC). They vary by age, sex, ethnicity, and region. These are reference guidelines; your personal medical history always takes precedence.

## How It's Calculated

Fasting blood glucose is classified as:
- **Normal**: <100 mg/dL
- **Prediabetes**: 100-125 mg/dL
- **Diabetes**: ≥126 mg/dL

This calculator applies the standard classification to your glucose reading, checking for typical ranges and alerting you to unusually high or low values.

## Normal Reference Values for Adults

| Parameter | Normal Range |
|---|---|
| Blood Pressure | <120/80 mmHg |
| Resting Heart Rate | 60-100 bpm |
| Body Temperature | 36.1-37.2°C |
| SpO2 | 95-100% |
| Fasting Glucose | 70-100 mg/dL |
| HbA1c | <5.7% |
| Total Cholesterol | <200 mg/dL |
| LDL Cholesterol | <100 mg/dL |
| HDL Cholesterol | >40 (M) / >50 (F) |

## Important Notes

This calculator is a reference tool only. For critical decisions (medical, financial, legal), consult a qualified healthcare professional. Values and formulas are current as of 2026 and reviewed periodically.

faq.q: What is a normal fasting blood glucose level?
faq.a: Normal fasting glucose is less than 100 mg/dL. Values of 100-125 mg/dL indicate prediabetes, and ≥126 mg/dL suggest diabetes. These thresholds are based on international guidelines from the WHO and CDC.

faq.q: How do I prepare for a fasting glucose test?
faq.a: Fast for 8-10 hours before the test (water is allowed). Avoid eating, drinking, and strenuous exercise during this period. Most fasting tests are done in the morning after an overnight fast.

faq.q: What does it mean if my fasting glucose is 110?
faq.a: A fasting glucose of 110 mg/dL falls in the prediabetes range (100-125 mg/dL). This is higher than normal but below the diabetes threshold. Consult your doctor about lifestyle changes and monitoring.

faq.q: Can medications affect fasting glucose levels?
faq.a: Yes, many medications (statins, beta-blockers, diuretics, corticosteroids) can affect glucose levels. Always inform your doctor of all medications before testing and discussing results.

faq.q: When should I see a doctor about elevated fasting glucose?
faq.a: See your doctor if your fasting glucose is consistently above 100 mg/dL, if you have symptoms of diabetes, or if you have a family history of diabetes or cardiovascular disease.

faq.q: Is fasting glucose testing accurate for diabetes diagnosis?
faq.a: Fasting glucose is a reliable screening tool, but diagnosis typically requires confirming results. Additional tests like HbA1c or oral glucose tolerance test may be needed for confirmation.

faq.q: What causes elevated fasting blood glucose?
faq.a: Common causes include insulin resistance, type 2 diabetes, stress, certain medications, pregnancy (gestational diabetes), and metabolic disorders. A doctor can help identify the underlying cause.

faq.q: Do fasting glucose levels differ between men and women?
faq.a: The standard reference ranges are the same for men and women. However, pregnancy can affect glucose metabolism, and women may develop gestational diabetes during pregnancy with different diagnostic criteria.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required
